content.problemStatement:
A string is almost palindromic if we can rearrange its characters in such a way that it becomes a palindrome. A positive integer is almost palindromic if its decimal representation without leading zeros is an almost palindromic string.

You are given two integers LLL and RRR. Find the number of almost palindromic integers between LLL and RRR (inclusive). Since this number could be very large, compute it modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7.
constraints: The sum of lengths of R over all test cases does not exceed 10^6
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T. Each test case contains two space-separated integers L and R (without leading zeros).
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer — the number of almost palindromic integers modulo 10^9 + 7.

solution.approach: We will use dynamic programming to solve this problem. We will first calculate the number of almost palindromic numbers for each length from 1 to 10^6. Then, for each test case, we will calculate the number of almost palindromic numbers between L and R by summing up the number of almost palindromic numbers for each length.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * 10^6) with T being the number of test cases, as we need to calculate the number of almost palindromic numbers for each length from 1 to 10^6.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(10^6) as we need to store the number of almost palindromic numbers for each length from 1 to 10^6.
